title: Multi-Table SQL & Joins
subtitle: Follow relationship paths, preserve unmatched rows, and build accurate reports across related tables.
description: A focused SQLite course with three standard modules and one final capstone. Learners move directly from single-table reporting into INNER JOIN, multi-table relationship paths, LEFT JOIN, anti-joins, and join-aware counting without repeating earlier SELECT, filtering, alias, aggregate, or GROUP BY lessons.
prerequisites: Write reliable SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, and LIMIT queries.
Use aliases, NULL checks, CASE, aggregate functions, GROUP BY, and HAVING.
State what one result row represents before validating a report.
Read table and column names in the schema browser.
intendedFor: Learners who completed SQL Foundations and SQL Analysis & Reporting and are ready to query normalized relational data.
learningGoals: Read primary-key and foreign-key relationships in a relational schema.
Write reliable INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN queries with explicit ON conditions.
Follow relationship paths through bridge tables across three or four tables.
Preserve unmatched rows and find missing relationships intentionally.
Count related rows and unique entities without duplicate inflation.
Complete three cumulative module projects and one integrated multi-table capstone.

courseOverview.recommendedSequence: Complete the three standard modules in order—join foundations, longer relationship paths, then outer joins and accurate counts—before the final capstone.
courseOverview.summary: Learners move directly from one-table reporting into relationship-driven SQL. The course is intentionally four modules so aliases, ON clauses, and individual join variants are not stretched into repetitive standalone modules.

moduleSummary.title: Join Foundations
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Read keys and write reliable two-table INNER JOIN queries.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Build a Student Enrollment Activity Roster.

moduleSummary.title: Following Relationship Paths
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Follow bridge tables across three and four related tables without accidental row multiplication.

moduleSummary.title: Outer Joins and Accurate Counts
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Preserve unmatched rows and calculate trustworthy relationship counts.

moduleSummary.title: Final Capstone
moduleSummary.coreSkill: Integrate relationship paths, LEFT JOIN, grain, and distinct counts in one final report.
moduleSummary.outputFocus: Complete the School Program Participation Report.

preferredTerms.relationship path: the key path connecting the requested tables

notes: This course has four modules total: three standard modules plus one final capstone. Do not expand it to five modules by splitting aliases, ON, or individual join variants into repetitive lessons.

Do not create standalone lessons that re-teach SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, aliases, aggregate functions, GROUP BY, HAVING, NULL, CASE, or deterministic sorting. Use those only as previously learned tools inside a new join problem.
Every technical lesson has exactly two distinct sketches and two aligned code_input Try It exercises.
Worked examples, Try It tasks, module projects, and capstone steps must use different relationship questions and output goals.
Each standard module has exactly one progressive module project. Only the final topic is rendered as a capstone.
A progressive SQL step contains one cumulative replacement SELECT statement. The next starter equals the previous full solution.
Every join must follow a documented school_relations_intro relationship. Never join columns merely because both are named id.
Teach report grain and row multiplication only in the context of joins; do not repeat the previous course general GROUP BY lessons.
Do not introduce subqueries, CTEs, window functions, self joins, CROSS JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL JOIN, or mutation SQL.
Use ERD and schema visuals for relationship decisions. All graded SQL must execute against school_relations_intro.

requiredDataCharacteristics: At least one student has no enrollment so LEFT JOIN and anti-join results are non-empty.
At least one course has no enrollment so missing-course audits are non-empty.
At least one department has no course so all-department LEFT JOIN reports include a zero-count row.
At least one student has multiple enrollments so row multiplication and COUNT(DISTINCT) can be demonstrated.
At least one department owns multiple courses so one-to-many grouping is meaningful.

globalRules: Every technical sketch must have a required embedded Try It.
Try It IDs and exercise keys must start with try- and follow try-<topic-id>-sketchN.
Every standard module must contain exactly one role=module_project section.
The final module must contain exactly one role=capstone section and one visible capstone topic.
Module project and capstone step N+1 starterCode must equal step N solutionCode.
Every JOIN must include an ON condition matching a declared relationship unless the exercise is an explicitly marked broken-query diagnosis.
Every expected result must be generated from school_relations_intro.
Every multi-table SELECT must qualify duplicate-prone source columns.
No topic may exist solely to re-teach a concept completed in SQL Foundations or SQL Analysis & Reporting.
When output order matters, expected rows must be order-stable and the query must include deterministic tie-breaking.

notes: Use only two-table INNER JOINs in teaching and project work.
The relationship-key topic may inspect tables separately before the first JOIN.
Teach longer INNER JOIN paths one documented relationship at a time.
Keep one result row per enrollment unless a prompt explicitly states another grain.
LEFT JOIN starts from the entity the report must preserve.
Counts must use right-side identifiers and DISTINCT only when uniqueness is requested.
The capstone integrates the complete course without adding a new SQL construct.
Every step preserves all departments and one-row-per-department grain.

modules.title: Join Foundations
modules.description: Read relationship keys and write reliable two-table INNER JOIN queries without re-teaching single-table SQL.
modules.purpose: Bridge from one-table reporting to relational queries by making each ON condition explicit and verifiable.
learningObjectives: Trace primary-key and foreign-key relationships in the ERD.
Use INNER JOIN and ON to connect matching rows.
Qualify columns when multiple tables contain similar names.
Use concise table aliases without hiding the relationship logic.
guidedExercises: Match course department_id values to department ids.
Repair ambiguous and unqualified joined columns.
Build a two-table student enrollment roster in five cumulative steps.
modules.moduleProject: Build a Student Enrollment Activity Roster from students and enrollments.

sections.title: Relationship Keys
sections.description: Use the schema and ERD to identify which columns can connect tables.

topics.title: Reading Keys and Relationships
topics.summary: Trace documented primary-key and foreign-key pairs before writing a join.
learningGoals: Identify the primary key on each table in the course dataset.
Match courses.department_id to departments.id and enrollment foreign keys to their parent tables.
Explain why columns with similar values are not automatically a valid relationship.

topics.title: INNER JOIN with ON
topics.summary: Connect matching rows with one explicit relationship condition.
learningGoals: Write an INNER JOIN between two related tables.
Place the key comparison in an ON clause.
Predict which unmatched rows an INNER JOIN removes.

sections.title: Readable Join Queries
sections.description: Keep joined queries unambiguous as more columns appear.

topics.title: Qualifying Columns Across Tables
topics.summary: Use table-qualified column references so SQL and readers know which source each value comes from.
learningGoals: Qualify selected columns with a table name or alias.
Resolve ambiguous id and name references.
Keep output aliases separate from source qualification.

topics.title: Table Aliases for Join Queries
topics.summary: Shorten table references while preserving a visible relationship path.
learningGoals: Assign concise aliases in FROM and JOIN clauses.
Use the same aliases consistently in SELECT and ON.
Choose aliases that remain readable in multi-line SQL.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.description: Build one dependable two-table roster without discarding earlier work.

topics.title: Module 0 Project: Student Enrollment Activity Roster
topics.summary: Build a qualified and readable student-enrollment roster through five cumulative join steps.
learningGoals: Preserve the correct students-to-enrollments relationship through every step.
Keep one result row per enrollment record.
Finish with exact aliases, a focused filter, and deterministic sorting.

projectBrief.scenario: The registrar needs a dependable enrollment activity list that connects each enrollment record to the correct student without duplicating or losing rows.
projectBrief.workspace: Browser SQL editor, schema browser, ERD, and deterministic results table
projectBrief.deliverable: One two-table enrollment roster with qualified columns, readable aliases, a grade-level filter, and stable ordering.
requirements: Keep one valid SELECT statement in every step.
Join students to enrollments only through students.id = enrollments.student_id.
Use the previous full solution as the next starter query.
Preserve every earlier requirement unless the current step explicitly replaces it.
Do not introduce LEFT JOIN, subqueries, CTEs, window functions, or mutation SQL.

stepLadder.title: Connect Students to Enrollment Records
stepLadder.requirement: Use an INNER JOIN to connect students and enrollments. Return student id, student name, enrollment id, course id, and term.

stepLadder.title: Qualify Every Shared Identifier
stepLadder.requirement: Keep the same rows and qualify every selected id column with its table name or alias.

stepLadder.title: Add Readable Table and Column Aliases
stepLadder.requirement: Use concise table aliases and rename the displayed identifiers as student_id, enrollment_id, and course_id.

stepLadder.title: Focus on Upper Grade Levels
stepLadder.requirement: Keep the complete joined roster and include only students in grade level 10 or higher.

stepLadder.title: Order the Roster Predictably
stepLadder.requirement: Sort by student name ascending, then course_id ascending, while preserving the complete query.

modules.title: Following Relationship Paths
modules.description: Move through bridge tables and extend a query across three or four related tables without creating accidental row combinations.
modules.purpose: Teach learners to reason about the path between requested fields rather than adding JOIN clauses by trial and error.
learningObjectives: Distinguish one-to-many and many-to-many result behavior.
Use enrollments as a bridge between students and courses.
Extend a valid join path one relationship at a time.
Detect missing, reversed, or unrelated ON conditions.
guidedExercises: Follow students through enrollments to courses.
Extend a three-table query to department details.
Diagnose a Cartesian result and a wrong-key join.
Build a four-table course enrollment directory.
modules.moduleProject: Build a Course Enrollment Directory across departments, courses, enrollments, and students.

sections.title: Relationship Shapes
sections.description: Understand how relationship cardinality changes the result rows.

topics.title: One-to-Many and Many-to-Many Results
topics.summary: Predict why one parent row may appear several times after joining to related detail rows.
learningGoals: Describe what one result row represents after a one-to-many join.
Explain why a student can appear once per enrollment.
Recognize enrollments as the bridge between students and courses.

topics.title: Joining Through a Bridge Table
topics.summary: Connect two entity tables through the enrollment records that relate them.
learningGoals: Join students to enrollments and then to courses.
Use one ON clause for each relationship in the path.
Keep the intended one-row-per-enrollment grain visible.

sections.title: Longer Join Paths
sections.description: Extend and debug longer relational queries.

topics.title: Extending a Join Path
topics.summary: Add one related table to an already-correct join without rewriting the query from scratch.
learningGoals: Extend students-enrollments-courses to departments.
Select descriptive columns from each table with clear qualification.
Verify that each added JOIN follows an ERD relationship.

topics.title: Avoiding Cartesian and Wrong-Key Joins
topics.summary: Diagnose results that multiply unexpectedly or match unrelated identifiers.
learningGoals: Recognize the symptoms of a missing ON clause.
Reject joins that compare unrelated id columns.
Use expected row grain and sample values to verify a join path.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.description: Build one four-table directory by extending a correct relationship path.

topics.title: Module 1 Project: Course Enrollment Directory
topics.summary: Build a department-to-student enrollment directory through five cumulative multi-table steps.
learningGoals: Preserve one result row per enrollment through every step.
Follow the complete documented relationship path.
Finish with exact aliases and deterministic directory ordering.

projectBrief.scenario: The academic office needs a course enrollment directory that shows who is taking each course and which department owns it.
projectBrief.workspace: Browser SQL editor, schema browser, ERD, and deterministic results table
projectBrief.deliverable: One four-table course enrollment directory with a correct relationship path and readable output.
requirements: Keep one valid SELECT statement in every step.
Follow the relationship path departments → courses → enrollments → students.
Use each ON clause for one documented key relationship.
Use the previous full solution as the next starter query.
Do not use LEFT JOIN, subqueries, CTEs, window functions, or mutation SQL.

stepLadder.title: Connect Courses to Departments
stepLadder.requirement: Join courses to departments and return course title with department name.

stepLadder.title: Add Enrollment Records
stepLadder.requirement: Extend the existing query through enrollments so each result row represents one enrollment in a course.

stepLadder.title: Add Student Details
stepLadder.requirement: Extend the same relationship path to students and return the student name and grade level.

stepLadder.title: Clarify the Directory Output
stepLadder.requirement: Use exact aliases department, course, student, grade_level, and term without changing the row grain.

stepLadder.title: Sort the Directory for Review
stepLadder.requirement: Order by department, course, and student ascending with deterministic output.

modules.title: Outer Joins and Accurate Counts
modules.description: Preserve unmatched rows, place filters safely, find missing relationships, and count related records without inflating metrics.
modules.purpose: Combine join reasoning with reporting skills from the previous course while focusing only on new multi-table failure modes.
learningObjectives: Use LEFT JOIN to preserve every row from the chosen starting table.
Explain how WHERE can accidentally remove unmatched LEFT JOIN rows.
Find entities with no related records using an anti-join pattern.
Use COUNT(right_id) and COUNT(DISTINCT ...) at the intended result grain.
guidedExercises: Keep all students while showing available enrollment details.
Compare a right-table condition in ON with the same condition in WHERE.
Find courses with no enrollments and departments with no courses.
Count courses, enrollment rows, and unique students by department.
modules.moduleProject: Build a Department Participation Scorecard that preserves inactive departments and reports accurate counts.

sections.title: Preserving Unmatched Rows
sections.description: Use LEFT JOIN intentionally and keep its unmatched rows visible.

topics.title: LEFT JOIN Preserves Unmatched Rows
topics.summary: Choose a starting table and keep every row even when no related record exists.
learningGoals: Identify the preserved left side of a LEFT JOIN.
Explain why unmatched right-side columns appear as NULL.
Choose INNER JOIN or LEFT JOIN from the report requirement.

topics.title: Filtering LEFT JOINs with ON and WHERE
topics.summary: Place right-table conditions according to whether unmatched left rows must survive.
learningGoals: Compare a right-table filter inside ON with the same filter in WHERE.
Explain why WHERE right_column = value can remove unmatched rows.
Preserve all left rows while limiting which right rows can match.

sections.title: Missing Relationships and Counts
sections.description: Turn unmatched rows into useful audits and reliable grouped metrics.

topics.title: Finding Missing Relationships
topics.summary: Use LEFT JOIN plus a right-side IS NULL check to locate missing related records.
learningGoals: Find students without enrollments.
Find courses without enrollment records.
Explain why the NULL test targets the joined table key.

topics.title: Counting Related Rows Without Inflation
topics.summary: Count matched rows and unique entities without allowing join multiplication to distort a report.
learningGoals: Use COUNT(right_table.id) so unmatched rows produce zero.
Use COUNT(DISTINCT entity_id) for unique-entity metrics.
State the grouped result grain before choosing a count expression.

sections.title: Module Project
sections.description: Build one all-department scorecard with trustworthy relationship counts.

topics.title: Module 2 Project: Department Participation Scorecard
topics.summary: Build an all-department scorecard with course, enrollment, and distinct-student counts in six cumulative steps.
learningGoals: Preserve every department through every LEFT JOIN.
Keep one result row per department.
Finish with accurate counts and deterministic ranking.

projectBrief.scenario: School leadership wants a department participation scorecard that includes departments with no courses and courses with no enrollments.
projectBrief.workspace: Browser SQL editor, schema browser, ERD, and deterministic results table
projectBrief.deliverable: One all-department scorecard with accurate course, enrollment, and distinct-student counts.
requirements: Begin from departments and preserve every department with LEFT JOIN.
Use COUNT on right-side identifiers so unmatched rows count as zero.
Use COUNT(DISTINCT ...) only where the requested metric requires unique entities.
State and preserve one result row per department.
Use the previous full solution as the next starter query.
Do not use subqueries, CTEs, window functions, or mutation SQL.

stepLadder.title: Preserve Every Department
stepLadder.requirement: Start from departments and LEFT JOIN courses so Arts remains visible even without a course.

stepLadder.title: Count Courses Correctly
stepLadder.requirement: Group at one row per department and add COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) as course_count so later enrollment joins cannot inflate the course total.

stepLadder.title: Extend to Enrollment Records
stepLadder.requirement: LEFT JOIN enrollments through courses while preserving all departments.

stepLadder.title: Count Enrollment Rows
stepLadder.requirement: Add COUNT(e.id) as enrollment_count without changing the department grain.

stepLadder.title: Count Unique Students
stepLadder.requirement: Add COUNT(DISTINCT e.student_id) as student_count so one student enrolled in multiple courses is counted once per department.

stepLadder.title: Rank the Scorecard Predictably
stepLadder.requirement: Sort by enrollment_count descending, then department ascending.

modules.title: Final Capstone
modules.description: Build one complete school-program participation report that preserves inactive departments and reports trustworthy multi-table metrics.
modules.purpose: Integrate relationship paths, LEFT JOIN, join-aware grain, and accurate counting in one portfolio-quality query.
learningObjectives: Plan a relationship path from requested output back to the required tables.
Preserve all departments while joining courses and enrollments.
Calculate course, enrollment, and unique-student counts at one-row-per-department grain.
Add a clear participation label and deterministic ranking.
guidedExercises: Preview the department-to-course relationship before the capstone.
Build the full report in four cumulative steps.
Validate unmatched departments, count logic, aliases, grain, and order.
modules.moduleProject: Build the final School Program Participation Report.

sections.title: Final Capstone
sections.description: Complete one focused multi-table report from brief to final validated query.

topics.title: Final Capstone: School Program Participation Report
topics.summary: Build one all-department participation report through a relationship-preview Try It and four cumulative capstone steps.
learningGoals: Preserve every department, including departments with no courses.
Keep one row per department while joining through courses to enrollments.
Calculate accurate related-row and distinct-student metrics.
Finish with a participation label and stable leadership-ready ranking.

projectBrief.scenario: The principal needs one compact school-program report that shows participation across every department, including inactive areas that would disappear in an INNER JOIN.
projectBrief.workspace: Browser SQL editor, schema browser, ERD, and deterministic results table
projectBrief.deliverable: A portfolio-quality all-department participation report built through one four-step cumulative query.
requirements: Preserve all departments from the first step through the final query.
Use the relationship path departments → courses → enrollments and count right-side identifiers.
Keep one row per department throughout the capstone.
Use the previous full solution as the next starter query.
Finish with exact aliases and deterministic ordering.
Do not use subqueries, CTEs, window functions, or mutation SQL.

stepLadder.title: Establish the All-Department Grain
stepLadder.requirement: Start from departments, LEFT JOIN courses and enrollments, and group so one result row represents one department.

stepLadder.title: Add Course and Enrollment Counts
stepLadder.requirement: Add course_count and enrollment_count using the correct right-side identifiers.

stepLadder.title: Add Distinct Student Participation
stepLadder.requirement: Add COUNT(DISTINCT e.student_id) as student_count without changing the grain.

stepLadder.title: Label and Rank Participation
stepLadder.requirement: Add participation_status with CASE: No activity when enrollment_count is zero, Broad participation when both course_count and student_count are at least 2, otherwise Active. Sort by enrollment_count descending and department ascending.

authoringGuidance: This course must always use subjectSlug sql, courseSlug multi-table-sql, catalogSlug sql, and profileId sql.
Use browser-sql-runner and sql-browser-workspace across every module. Learners write in the SQL editor, click Run query, and inspect the results table.
Use school_relations_intro in every module and expose schema, ERD, and Chen relationship visuals.
Assume learners already know SELECT, filtering, sorting, aliases, NULL, CASE, aggregates, GROUP BY, HAVING, and deterministic ordering. Never create standalone review topics for them.
Every technical sketch must have a required embedded Try It code_input with tryItPlacement all_sketches.
Embedded Try It IDs, tryIt.id, and tryIt.exerciseKey must use try-<topic-id>-sketchN.
Quizzes are conceptual and must not use code_input; graded SQL belongs in Try It, module projects, or capstone steps.
Each standard module has exactly one progressive module project. The final module contains exactly one capstone topic.
Project and capstone step N+1 starterCode must equal step N full solutionCode. Solutions are complete snapshots, never diffs.
Every JOIN follows a declared foreign-key relationship. Broken-query examples must be explicitly labeled as diagnosis tasks and must not become solution queries.
Use table qualification whenever two source tables contain id or name columns.
State what one result row represents whenever a join can multiply parent rows.
Examples, Try Its, projects, and capstone steps must use distinct questions. Do not repeat the same join with only renamed aliases.
Do not teach subqueries in this course. Reserve subqueries, CTEs, set operations, and window functions for Advanced SQL Analytics.
Do not use terminal, command-line, sqlite3, psql, SQL file, or run-file instructions.

assessmentAndDelivery.suggestedBeginnerRhythm: Complete one standard module per week, including its cumulative project, then reserve a final session for the capstone.
recommendedCourseDeliverables: Embedded Try It query on every technical sketch

moduleMilestones.learnersCan: Read relationship keys and write qualified INNER JOIN queries.
Join through bridge tables across three or four tables and verify row grain.
Preserve unmatched rows, audit missing relationships, and count related records accurately.
Build and validate one complete all-department participation report.

assessmentAndDelivery.closingNote: Learners finish ready for SQL Data Management, where they will create and safely modify relational data rather than only query it.

notes: Teach only new relationship and join reasoning.
Keep project work to students and enrollments.
Extend one relationship at a time.
Use expected grain to detect accidental combinations.
Start from the entity the report must preserve.
Count right-side identifiers rather than COUNT(*).
Add no new SQL construct in the capstone.
Preserve all departments and one-row-per-department grain.
